quote: yeah. The the government, they don't pay for solar, unfortunately.
stewart_read: This is the highest-leverage flip opportunity in the call. Harry's 'freebie' frame is not ignorance — it's a cost-pain signal in disguise. He wants solar to be free because he doesn't want to pay his current bill either. The rep's 'unfortunately they don't pay for solar' closes the door on the frame instead of walking through it. The inversion was available: his current bill IS the cost he's already paying, every month, with no asset at the end. Reactive read holds — there is no evidence the rep was setting up a later move; the call never returned to this frame.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was deliberately clearing the misconception before building value — a reasonable sequencing choice.
coaching_implication: Show Holland this exact line in the 1-on-1. Ask: 'What was Harry actually telling you about his bill?' Then walk through the inversion structure from coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion. The suggested move is not a script line — it's a redirect: acknowledge the freebie wish, then pivot to what he IS paying right now and what that adds up to over time. No fabricated numbers needed; the pivot is emotional, not mathematical.

quote: I'm I'm I'm a mechanical engineer, and I'm thinking, hey. I have solar lights all around my patio, and they work really good. Why can't I put a portable solar field in my backyard, which is huge, and feed it into my house?
stewart_read: Harry volunteers two high-value signals in one breath: (1) he has a 'huge' backyard — a ground-mount feasibility signal that a closer could use to design a larger system than a roof-only install would support; (2) he is an engineer who has already thought through DIY solar, which means his interest is genuine and his engagement with a real design would be high. The rep responds only to the business-model question ('we don't sell equipment like that') and misses both signals entirely.
coaching_implication: The 'huge backyard' line is a closer-handoff note even if the call had ended in a booking. In the 1-on-1, ask Holland: 'What did Harry just tell you about his property?' The coaching move is to teach Holland to treat engineering curiosity as a buying signal — Harry is not objecting, he's designing. The bridge is: 'That's exactly the kind of thing our specialist maps out — they'll look at your whole property, not just the roof.'

quote: So we we actually we don't sell, like, the equipment. Like, we don't just sell our equipment like that. We only sell the solar system, and then we we will come out and and install it and then everything.
stewart_read: Three utterances explaining what Ion does and doesn't sell. Per setter_scope_discipline.scope_creep_signals, 'rep debating company operations' is a named anti-pattern. The rep is not selling solar here — she's defending Ion's business model to a customer who asked a genuine question. The universal escape hatch ('that's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer') was available and unused. Reactive read holds — this is mild scope creep, not a strategic setup.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was briefly clarifying to prevent Harry from pursuing a dead-end DIY path before bridging to the appointment.
coaching_implication: This is a low-severity instance but worth naming because it compounds with the other reactive moments. In the 1-on-1, contrast this with the escape hatch from setter_scope_discipline: 'I'm scheduling, they're solving.' One sentence, then redirect to the appointment. Holland spent three utterances where one would have done.

quote: Yeah. I know. The the calls definitely get old. I agree.
stewart_read: Harry has just described a world of AI-generated scam content impersonating celebrities to sell supplements — a general societal anxiety, not a personal scam disclosure. The rep commiserates ('I agree') rather than using the moment to distinguish Ion from the noise. The scam-anxiety signal is present even without a personal encounter: Harry is telling the rep that he is primed to distrust unsolicited contact. The correct move is to reframe the appointment as customer-initiated — Harry filled out a form, Ion is responding to his request, not cold-calling him. Commiseration validates his distrust without resolving it.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was building rapport by agreeing with Harry's frustration before pivoting to a reframe — a reasonable sequencing choice if the pivot had followed.
coaching_implication: Show Holland the 02:26–03:15 sequence as a unit. Harry's contact-volume complaint and AI/scam tangent are the same anxiety in two forms. The reframe is simple and grounded in fact: 'You actually reached out to us — you filled out a form, so this isn't a cold call.' That one sentence changes the frame from 'another unsolicited caller' to 'I'm responding to what you asked for.' Holland never made that move.

quote: alright. Well, anyway, the answer is no. I'm not interested.
stewart_read: Harry's close is a soft refusal — 'anyway' signals he's wrapping up a tangent, not delivering a considered decision. The schema's changed_mind rebuttal is directly applicable: 'Just curious — did you get a chance to look at the actual design with the numbers yet?' Harry has never seen a design from Ion. The rebuttal's logic ('most people tell me they're not interested until they see how much they could actually save') fits precisely. Rep accepts the refusal with 'Okay' and moves to list removal. Reactive read holds — no evidence of strategic deferral.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep judged Harry as genuinely unqualified (no bill, no homeowner confirmation, cost-skeptical) and made a time-efficiency call to release the lead.
coaching_implication: This is the call's clearest coachable moment. In the 1-on-1, play the 03:15–03:24 clip and ask Holland: 'What rebuttal did you have available here?' Walk through the changed_mind structure. The goal is not to argue with Harry — it's to create one crack of curiosity before releasing. Holland didn't attempt it once.

quote: To be honest, I'm not too sure, like, what the pricing looks like because I'm just a scheduler.
stewart_read: The scope-hold here is technically correct — pricing is closer-tier content. But 'I'm just a scheduler' is self-deprecating framing that reduces Holland's authority and does nothing to create curiosity about what the closer CAN show Harry. The escape hatch from setter_scope_discipline ('I'm scheduling, they're solving') holds the scope without diminishing the role. The knowledge gap is not in the information — it's in how Holland frames her own position.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the 01:48 scope-creep clip. Holland oscillates between over-explaining Ion's model and underselling her own role. The coaching target is a single confident bridge line that holds scope AND creates curiosity. Suggested intent (not verbatim): 'Pricing depends on your specific design — that's exactly what the specialist builds for you. Let me get you on the calendar.'

quote: I did look at, Sunrun for a while about six, eight months ago, and I rejected and I rejected putting them in at that time.
stewart_read: Harry volunteers a prior Sunrun engagement that he personally rejected. This is a high-value signal: he was interested enough to go through a design process, he made the decision himself (not a spouse veto), and he walked away — meaning he has unresolved objections the closer could address. The rep acknowledges with 'Mhmm' and moves immediately to the tax credit correction. The already_saw_design rebuttal ('it's always best to get a couple looks so you have something to compare against') was available and unused. No competitive context was captured.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was prioritizing the government-funding misconception as the more urgent frame to correct before circling back to the Sunrun history.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Holland: 'What did Harry just tell you about why he didn't go with Sunrun?' She doesn't know — she never asked. The Sunrun rejection is the most useful piece of competitive intelligence on this call. Teaching Holland to pause on 'I rejected them' and ask 'What made you decide against it?' would have surfaced Harry's real objection and given the closer a specific angle to work.
